lessons.title: std::print and std::println: Type-Safe Output
lessons.motivation: Before C++23, programmers faced two uncomfortable extremes: fast and concise printf — but unsafe (a mismatched format specifier causes undefined behavior), or verbose std::cout with chained << operators — type-safe but slow to compile and incompatible with format strings. C++20 brought std::format, combining type safety with a convenient format-string syntax, but it returned an intermediate std::string that still had to be passed to std::cout. std::print in C++23 closes that gap: it is std::format plus direct output, with no unnecessary allocations.
lessons.theory:
std::print and std::println are declared in the <print> header and share the same format-string syntax as std::format: {}, {:>10}, {:.2f}, and other specifiers work unchanged. std::print(fmt, args...) writes the formatted string to stdout without a trailing newline; std::println(fmt, args...) appends \n at the end. Both functions have an overload whose first parameter is FILE*: std::println(stderr, "err: {}", msg) directs output to the standard error stream or any other open FILE*.

Why did std::print not arrive alongside std::format in C++20? The reason is encoding on Windows. printf on that platform does not transcode UTF-8 to the UTF-16 expected by the console API, so non-ASCII characters — Cyrillic, emoji, accented letters — come out garbled without additional code-page gymnastics. The committee chose to wait for a standardized solution: std::print is required to correctly transcode Unicode when writing to a console on all platforms.

Under the hood, std::print relies on two low-level helpers: std::vprint_unicode — for streams where Unicode correctness matters (consoles, terminals) — and std::vprint_nonunicode — for binary or plain-text streams where transcoding is unnecessary. Most users never need them directly: std::print and std::println select the right variant automatically.

The practical migration rule is straightforward: replace printf("x = %d\n", x) with std::println("x = {}", x), and fprintf(stderr, "err: %s\n", msg) with std::println(stderr, "err: {}", msg). No manual type-based format control, no unsafe specifiers — the compiler checks argument types at compile time.

examples.title: Basic std::print and std::println usage
examples.description: Printing a string, an integer, and a floating-point value using various format specifiers.
examples.expectedOutput:
Name: Alice
Age:    30
Score: 98.5
No newline here — same line

examples.title: Alignment, zero-padding, and alternate bases
examples.description: Aligning text into columns, printing numbers with leading zeros, and displaying values in hexadecimal and octal — all through the unified format-string syntax.
examples.expectedOutput:
left            right   center  
Item 01:     3.14
Item 02:     6.28
Item 03:     9.42
Dec: 255  Hex: FF  Oct: 377
